content.problemStatement:
There were NNN students (numbered 111 through NNN) participating in the Indian Programming Camp (IPC) and they watched a total of KKK lectures (numbered 111 through KKK). For each student iii and each lecture jjj, the iii-th student watched the jjj-th lecture for Ti,jT_{i, j}Ti,j​ minutes.

Additionally, for each student iii, we know that this student asked the question, "What is the criteria for getting a certificate?" QiQ_iQi​ times.

The criteria for getting a certificate is that a student must have watched at least MMM minutes of lectures in total and they must have asked the question no more than 101010 times.

Find out how many participants are eligible for a certificate.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains three space‑separated integers N, M and K — the number of participants, the minimum total minutes required for a certificate and the number of lectures. Then N lines follow. The i‑th of these lines contains K+1 integers: Ti,1 Ti,2 … Ti,K Qi — the minutes the i‑th participant watched each lecture and the number of times they asked the question.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing one integer — the number of participants that satisfy both conditions (total watched minutes ≥ M and questions asked ≤ 10).

sampleTestCases.explanation: Only the second participant satisfies both conditions (total minutes = 12 ≥ 8 and questions = 4 ≤ 10).
First participant has enough minutes (6) but asks 11 questions (>10). Second participant asks only 5 questions but watches 4 minutes (<5). No one is eligible.
Only the first participant reaches 10 minutes and asks ≤10 questions.

hidden.label: edge case – both fail for different reasons

solution.approach: For each test case we iterate over all participants. While reading a participant we accumulate the sum of the K lecture times. After the K values we read Qi. The participant is eligible if sum ≥ M and Qi ≤ 10. We increase a counter for eligible participants and finally output the counter. The algorithm uses only a few integer variables besides the input stream.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T · N · K) – each lecture time is read exactly once.
